APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper squarely addresses an NLP problem (Open Information Extraction) appropriate for a top-tier NLP/ML conference and is targeted at the right audience. It uses standard datasets (NYT, Wikipedia), compares to established OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE), and reports common evaluation metrics (precision, recall, extraction length, attribution precision), matching community expectations. The methodology (implementation on ClausIE, explicit annotation of polarity/modality/attribution, multiple minimization modes) and availability of code and thorough related work place it well within the venue's scope despite acknowledged limitations in parsing and NER. | B(5): This paper is squarely within the NLP/Open Information Extraction topic and targets the right audience for a top NLP/ML venue by proposing a practical OIE system (MinIE) and comparing it to established baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE). The problem formulation and methods (relation enrichment, minimization modes, annotations for polarity/modality/attribution) are appropriate, and the evaluation uses standard datasets (NYT, Wikipedia), multiple relevant metrics (precision, recall, redundancy, extraction length), and ablation-like comparisons across modes; code is available. Although hyperparameters are not reported and some limitations are noted, these are minor presentation issues and do not undermine the paper’s relevance or fit for a top-tier conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper squarely addresses an NLP problem (Open Information Extraction) appropriate for a top-tier NLP/ML conference and is targeted at the right audience. It uses standard datasets (NYT, Wikipedia), compares to established OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE), and reports common evaluation metrics (precision, recall, extraction length, attribution precision), matching community expectations. The methodology (implementation on ClausIE, explicit annotation of polarity/modality/attribution, multiple minimization modes) and availability of code and thorough related work place it well within the venue's scope despite acknowledged limitations in parsing and NER.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is squarely within the NLP/Open Information Extraction topic and targets the right audience for a top NLP/ML venue by proposing a practical OIE system (MinIE) and comparing it to established baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE). The problem formulation and methods (relation enrichment, minimization modes, annotations for polarity/modality/attribution) are appropriate, and the evaluation uses standard datasets (NYT, Wikipedia), multiple relevant metrics (precision, recall, redundancy, extraction length), and ablation-like comparisons across modes; code is available. Although hyperparameters are not reported and some limitations are noted, these are minor presentation issues and do not undermine the paper’s relevance or fit for a top-tier conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): MinIE proposes a clear new perspective for OIE by minimizing extractions and encoding removed information (polarity, modality, attribution, quantities) as annotations, which is different from prior systems like ClausIE, OLLIE, and Stanford OIE. The work builds on ClausIE but adds non-trivial components (implicit-extraction, relation enrichment, a basic minimization step, and multiple minimization modes MinIE-S/F/C/A) and clearly differentiates these contributions. Overall this is a notable and informative extension of existing OIE methods rather than a purely incremental or purely radical departure. | B(4): The paper presents a clear new perspective for Open IE by explicitly minimizing extractions and preserving removed information as annotations (polarity, modality, attribution, quantities), which is not the focus of prior systems. Although implemented on top of an existing extractor (ClausIE) and combining multiple known components (implicit fact extraction, relation enrichment), the design of multiple minimization modes (MinIE-S/F/C/A) and the annotated-output approach constitute a non-trivial and well-differentiated extension. Given the reported competitive precision/recall and emphasis on much shorter extractions, the contribution would be notable to experts even if largely building on existing methods.

reviewer_a.justification: MinIE proposes a clear new perspective for OIE by minimizing extractions and encoding removed information (polarity, modality, attribution, quantities) as annotations, which is different from prior systems like ClausIE, OLLIE, and Stanford OIE. The work builds on ClausIE but adds non-trivial components (implicit-extraction, relation enrichment, a basic minimization step, and multiple minimization modes MinIE-S/F/C/A) and clearly differentiates these contributions. Overall this is a notable and informative extension of existing OIE methods rather than a purely incremental or purely radical departure.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a clear new perspective for Open IE by explicitly minimizing extractions and preserving removed information as annotations (polarity, modality, attribution, quantities), which is not the focus of prior systems. Although implemented on top of an existing extractor (ClausIE) and combining multiple known components (implicit fact extraction, relation enrichment), the design of multiple minimization modes (MinIE-S/F/C/A) and the annotated-output approach constitute a non-trivial and well-differentiated extension. Given the reported competitive precision/recall and emphasis on much shorter extractions, the contribution would be notable to experts even if largely building on existing methods.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem in OIE—producing concise, minimally worded extractions while preserving removed information as annotations—and demonstrates competitive or higher precision and recall than prior systems (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on NYT and Wiki samples while producing much shorter extractions. Its design (multiple minimization modes, explicit polarity/modality/attribution/quantity annotations, implementation on ClausIE, and available code) is likely to change how researchers balance brevity versus information retention and to be adopted or extended in downstream IE and KB construction work. It also opens clear follow-up directions (better parsing/NER integration, richer modality/attribution handling), though practical impact is moderated by limitations the authors note (dependency-parsing and NER errors, coarse modality modeling). | B(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem in OIE—producing concise, minimized extractions while preserving removed information via annotations (polarity, modality, attribution, quantities)—and implements this on top of ClausIE with an explicit minimization pipeline. Empirically it reports competitive or higher precision and recall than prior systems while producing much shorter extractions, which could change how practitioners design extraction pipelines and downstream consumers handle brevity vs. completeness. By providing code and multiple minimization modes the work opens new directions for annotated, loss-aware extraction, though known limitations (dependency-parsing errors, collocation and NER failures, and precision loss in aggressive mode) temper its immediate transformative potential.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem in OIE—producing concise, minimally worded extractions while preserving removed information as annotations—and demonstrates competitive or higher precision and recall than prior systems (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on NYT and Wiki samples while producing much shorter extractions. Its design (multiple minimization modes, explicit polarity/modality/attribution/quantity annotations, implementation on ClausIE, and available code) is likely to change how researchers balance brevity versus information retention and to be adopted or extended in downstream IE and KB construction work. It also opens clear follow-up directions (better parsing/NER integration, richer modality/attribution handling), though practical impact is moderated by limitations the authors note (dependency-parsing and NER errors, coarse modality modeling).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem in OIE—producing concise, minimized extractions while preserving removed information via annotations (polarity, modality, attribution, quantities)—and implements this on top of ClausIE with an explicit minimization pipeline. Empirically it reports competitive or higher precision and recall than prior systems while producing much shorter extractions, which could change how practitioners design extraction pipelines and downstream consumers handle brevity vs. completeness. By providing code and multiple minimization modes the work opens new directions for annotated, loss-aware extraction, though known limitations (dependency-parsing errors, collocation and NER failures, and precision loss in aggressive mode) temper its immediate transformative potential.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experimental evaluation is reasonably comprehensive: comparisons to ClausIE, OLLIE, and Stanford OIE on NYT and Wiki samples (and a large NYT-10k sample) with multiple precision and redundancy metrics support the main claims about shorter, high-precision extractions. The paper provides multiple minimization modes (MinIE-S/F/C/A) and discusses failure cases and limitations (parser and NER errors, collocation issues, modality scope), and code is available. However, no hyperparameters are reported and there is limited explicit ablation/sensitivity analysis isolating the contribution of components (e.g., implicit-extraction, relation enrichment), so reproducibility and component-wise attribution are somewhat under-addressed. | B(4): The experiments compare MinIE against multiple strong baselines on a large NYT-10k set and smaller NYT/Wiki samples, report precision/recall, attribution precision, redundancy and extraction length, and show trade-offs across multiple minimization modes—supporting the main claims. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported (e.g., effect of individual components or hyperparameters), hyperparameters are not given, and evaluation is limited to news/Wikipedia samples. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations (parsing, NER, modality, mode safety) are discussed, but some reproducibility details are missing despite released code.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is reasonably comprehensive: comparisons to ClausIE, OLLIE, and Stanford OIE on NYT and Wiki samples (and a large NYT-10k sample) with multiple precision and redundancy metrics support the main claims about shorter, high-precision extractions. The paper provides multiple minimization modes (MinIE-S/F/C/A) and discusses failure cases and limitations (parser and NER errors, collocation issues, modality scope), and code is available. However, no hyperparameters are reported and there is limited explicit ablation/sensitivity analysis isolating the contribution of components (e.g., implicit-extraction, relation enrichment), so reproducibility and component-wise attribution are somewhat under-addressed.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare MinIE against multiple strong baselines on a large NYT-10k set and smaller NYT/Wiki samples, report precision/recall, attribution precision, redundancy and extraction length, and show trade-offs across multiple minimization modes—supporting the main claims. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported (e.g., effect of individual components or hyperparameters), hyperparameters are not given, and evaluation is limited to news/Wikipedia samples. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations (parsing, NER, modality, mode safety) are discussed, but some reproducibility details are missing despite released code.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (clear pipeline: ClausIE input, enrichment, minimization modes, experiments on NYT/Wiki) and the main contributions are stated up front (MinIE’s minimization and annotation approach). Notation and definitions appear consistent and the evaluation tables/examples (precision/recall, µ/σ, redundancy) are informative. Writing quality is described as good, though minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., hyperparameters not reported and some limitations could be explained with more detail). | B(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a clear pipeline (ClausIE + implicit extraction + relation enrichment + minimization), and its main contributions are stated early (MinIE variants, annotations, competitiveness vs. baselines). Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, and the experimental presentation includes explicit metrics (precision/recall, attribution precision, mean word counts) making results interpretable. A minor presentation weakness is that some implementation/reporting details (e.g., hyperparameters not reported and notation specifics are not mentioned in the summary), which slightly reduces reproducibility/clarity but does not impede understanding overall.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (clear pipeline: ClausIE input, enrichment, minimization modes, experiments on NYT/Wiki) and the main contributions are stated up front (MinIE’s minimization and annotation approach). Notation and definitions appear consistent and the evaluation tables/examples (precision/recall, µ/σ, redundancy) are informative. Writing quality is described as good, though minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., hyperparameters not reported and some limitations could be explained with more detail).

Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough, and the experimental presentation includes explicit metrics (precision/recall, attribution precision, mean word counts) making results interpretable. A minor presentation weakness is that some implementation/reporting details (e.g., hyperparameters not reported and notation specifics are not mentioned in the summary), which slightly reduces reproducibility/clarity but does not impede understanding overall.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper directly compares MinIE to appropriate and recent OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on multiple datasets (NYT-10k, NYT, Wiki) and reports standard metrics (precision, recall, attribution precision, and extraction length), and related work coverage is described as thorough. It also acknowledges limitations (e.g., precision drop for aggressive mode) and claims competitive or higher precision/recall while producing shorter extractions. Minor gaps remain: the summary does not make explicit that all baselines were evaluated under identical conditions or detail when prior methods outperform MinIE, so comparisons appear good but not exhaustively documented. | B(4): The paper compares against standard OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on the same NYT/Wiki data samples and reports compatible metrics (precision/recall, attribution precision, redundancy, extraction length), so comparisons appear appropriate and fair. The authors acknowledge limitations and trade-offs (e.g., MinIE-A reduces length at a notable precision cost; parser/NER errors affect results) and provide thorough related-work coverage. Differences between MinIE and baselines (minimization modes, annotation output) are clearly described and analyzed. Given this, the comparisons are good with only minor omissions possible but not evident from the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper directly compares MinIE to appropriate and recent OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on multiple datasets (NYT-10k, NYT, Wiki) and reports standard metrics (precision, recall, attribution precision, and extraction length), and related work coverage is described as thorough. It also acknowledges limitations (e.g., precision drop for aggressive mode) and claims competitive or higher precision/recall while producing shorter extractions. Minor gaps remain: the summary does not make explicit that all baselines were evaluated under identical conditions or detail when prior methods outperform MinIE, so comparisons appear good but not exhaustively documented.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against standard OIE baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on the same NYT/Wiki data samples and reports compatible metrics (precision/recall, attribution precision, redundancy, extraction length), so comparisons appear appropriate and fair. The authors acknowledge limitations and trade-offs (e.g., MinIE-A reduces length at a notable precision cost; parser/NER errors affect results) and provide thorough related-work coverage. Differences between MinIE and baselines (minimization modes, annotation output) are clearly described and analyzed. Given this, the comparisons are good with only minor omissions possible but not evident from the summary.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental comparisons against reasonable baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on NYT-10k and smaller NYT/Wiki samples showing competitive precision/recall and shorter extractions. However, there are methodological weaknesses: the summary indicates an inconsistent use of the term “recall” (counting total extractions), hyperparameters are not reported, and no statistical tests are mentioned, which weakens confidence in some experimental conclusions. The authors explicitly acknowledge important error sources (dependency parsing, NER, collocations) and limitations of modes, so while these are minor issues that do not fatally undermine the results, they justify a deduction from a perfect score. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are supported by implementation details and empirical comparisons against ClausIE, OLLIE, and Stanford OIE on NYT and Wikipedia samples, reporting precision/recall and extraction-length metrics and providing code. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check, and none are required for the contribution. The experimental methodology is largely appropriate (relevant baselines and multiple datasets), but hyperparameters are not reported, some evaluations use small samples (200 sentences), and no statistical significance testing is mentioned. The authors acknowledge key limitations (dependency parsing, NER, modality detection, and safety caveats), so the conclusions are reasonable though experimental-reporting gaps prevent a technically flawless rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental comparisons against reasonable baselines (ClausIE, OLLIE, Stanford OIE) on NYT-10k and smaller NYT/Wiki samples showing competitive precision/recall and shorter extractions. However, there are methodological weaknesses: the summary indicates an inconsistent use of the term “recall” (counting total extractions), hyperparameters are not reported, and no statistical tests are mentioned, which weakens confidence in some experimental conclusions. The authors explicitly acknowledge important error sources (dependency parsing, NER, collocations) and limitations of modes, so while these are minor issues that do not fatally undermine the results, they justify a deduction from a perfect score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are supported by implementation details and empirical comparisons against ClausIE, OLLIE, and Stanford OIE on NYT and Wikipedia samples, reporting precision/recall and extraction-length metrics and providing code. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check, and none are required for the contribution. The experimental methodology is largely appropriate (relevant baselines and multiple datasets), but hyperparameters are not reported, some evaluations use small samples (200 sentences), and no statistical significance testing is mentioned. The authors acknowledge key limitations (dependency parsing, NER, modality detection, and safety caveats), so the conclusions are reasonable though experimental-reporting gaps prevent a technically flawless rating.

main_claims: MinIE produces minimized, annotated open information extraction (OIE) extractions by removing overly-specific parts and representing polarity, modality, attribution, and quantities as annotations.

MinIE is implemented on top of ClausIE and includes an extractor for implicit facts, relation enrichment, a basic minimization step, and multiple minimization modes (MinIE-S, MinIE-F, MinIE-C, MinIE-A) with varying aggressiveness.
MinIE achieves competitive or higher precision and recall than most prior systems while producing much shorter extractions.
MinIE can output annotations (e.g., polarity, modality, original relation, quantity) to retain information removed during minimization.
_summary.methodology: MinIE runs ClausIE plus an implicit-extraction component to obtain input extractions, enriches relations, rewrites extractions by detecting and removing polarity, modality, attribution, and quantities into basic extractions, and then applies one of several minimization modes (safe, frequency, collocations, aggressive) to produce minimized, annotated extractions.
datasets: NYT-10k (random sample of 10,000 sentences from the New York Times Annotated Corpus)
NYT (random sample of 200 sentences from the New York Times Annotated Corpus)
Wiki (random sample of 200 sentences from Wikipedia)
metrics: attribution precision (fraction of correct triples with correct attributions)
mean word count per extraction (µ) and standard deviation (σ)
limitations: Errors in dependency parsing transfer to MinIE and are a main source of errors (affecting MinIE-S).
MinIE-C and MinIE-F can drop adjectives that form collocations when those collocations are not present in the dictionary, breaking up valid collocations.
Named-entity recognition errors can cause incorrect extractions (e.g., misrecognized entity boundaries).
The modality detection focuses on easy cases and only uses two levels (CT and PS).
The safe mode is not safe when applied to input extractions (only safe when applied to basic extractions).
The aggressive mode (MinIE-A) reduces extraction length but leads to a notable drop in precision.
